text: There is a telling difference between the calendars of prolific writers and those of busy executives: the writers' calendars are mostly empty. Annie Dillard, who wrote about the discipline of creative work in 'The Writing Life,' described her ideal day as a single long session in a bare room with no telephone. Not because she was antisocial, but because she understood that the self who walks into a rich social environment and the self who can sustain a 2,000-word argument are mutually exclusive on any given morning. Protecting a creative block is an act of identity management as much as time management — it is a decision about which self shows up to do the work.
question: According to the passage, what does Annie Dillard's approach to a working day reveal about creative blocks?

options.text: Long writing sessions require complete silence and the absence of all technology.
Prolific writers are fundamentally more introverted than executives.
Protecting creative blocks is an act of identity management, not just time management.
Writing 2,000 words per day requires at least eight hours of uninterrupted solitude.
